Lat., minutely toothed; (1) for the leaves. (Ceropegia, Cheiridopsis, Cras- sula lanceolata ssp.) - (2) for the petal tips. (Delosperma)
Lat. 'dentieulus', small tooth; and Lat. '-fer, -fera, -ferum', -carrying; for leaf- margins. (Haworthia chloracantha var.)
From Lat. 'dentire', becoming teeth; for the small leaf margin teeth. (Agave cer- ulata ssp.)
For William C. Denton (1886 or 1887-1953), English sueculent plant hobby- ist who created the hybrid. (Euphorbia)
Lat., denuded, stripped, worn off; for the few appressed spines. (Gymnocalyci- um)
Lat., depauperate; for the small 'starved' habit. iOpuntia, Sedum radiatum ssp.)
Lat., suspended, hanging down; (1) for the growth form. (Chasmanthera, Cleistocactus, Crassula, Quaqua) - (2) for the branches. (Lampranthus)

Lat., depressed, flattened; (1) for the low shrubby growth. tOpuntia, Ruschlai - (2) for the leaves appressed to the ground. (Glottiphyllum) - (3) for the flattened fused leaf pairs. (Conophytum) - (4) perhaps for the flattish inflorescence, or for the flatly spreading leaves. (Mas sonia) - (5) applica- tion obscure. (Aloe br evifolia var., Crassula)

For the occurrence near De Rust, Western Cape, RSA. (Haworthia blackbur- niae var.)

Lat., of the desert. (Agave , Aloe, Esco- baria)

As above. (Jatropha, Portulaca)
For the occurrence in the Dhufar [Dhofar] Prov., Oman. (Aloe)

For the genus Dianthus ("Pinks"; Caryophyllaceae); and Lat. '-flo- rus', -flowered; for the fringed petals. (Al so- bia) diaz-romeroanus For Dr. Belisario Dfaz Romero (fl. 1920), Bolivian naturalist. (Pe - reskia)

Lat., dichotomous, division in pairs; (1) for the branching of the sterns. (Aloe) - (2) for the occasional apparently dichotomous branching. tCeropegia, Cras- sula) - (3) for the branching of the inflores- cence. (Eberlanzia)

Gr. 'dikros', fork; and Gr. 'kau- los', stern; because the long shoots are over- topped by lateral shoots after flowering, giv- ing the appearance of a fork. (Aizoa ceae)
Gr. 'diktyon', net; and Gr. 'an- thos', flower; for the pattern of the corolla. (Matelea)
Gr. 'di', double; and Gr. 'delta', the letter D or a triangle; perhaps for the double involucral triangular scales (Jackson 1990). (Asteraceae) Didierea
For Col. Alfred Grandidier (1836- 1921), French pioneer explorer and chroni- cler of Madagascar. (Didiereaceae)

Gr. 'didymos', twice, double; and Gr. 'aotos', flower; for the laterally ap- pearing flowers, one on each side of the plant. (Aizoaceae)

For Carlos M. Diego Legrand (* 1901), Uruguayan botanist and specialist of the ge- nus Portulaca. (Portulaca)
For Prof. Dr. Friedrich L. E. Diels (1874-1945), German botanist in Berlin and at some time director of the botanical gar- den Berlin. tHaworthia- cooperi var., Ru- schia)

Nach Prof. Lothar Diers (fl. 1981), German cactus specialist in Köln. (Piloso- cereus)
For Jennie van Ackeren Dieterle (* 1909), US-American botanist at the Uni- versity of Michigan Herbarium and Cucur- bitaceae specialist. (Parasicyos)
Lat., difficult; (1) probably for the difficulty in classifying the taxon. (Aspido- glossum) - (2) for the difficulty to cultivate the plants. (Coryphantha)
Lat., unevenly or differently formed, unlike what is usual; (1) for the dimorphie leaves. (Rabiea, Stomatium) - (2) for the appearance. (Agave, Glottiphyllum)
Lat., breaking into pieces, shatter- ing; for the easily detached bracts. (Echeve- ria)

Lat., diffuse, widely spreading, scat- tered; (1) for the growth form. (Caralluma, Praecereus euchlorus ssp., Sedum, Trianth- ema) - (2) for the low, f1at, ill-defined tuber- cles of the plant bodies. (Lophophora) - (3) for the scattered f1owers. (Villadia)
Lat., digitate; (1) for the digitate leaves. (Adansonia, Adenia, Cucurbita) - (2) for the position of the two different 66 leaves of a pair in a "finger and thumb" manner. (Phyllobolus)
Lat. 'digitus', finger; and Gr. 'stigma', stigma, spot, dot; for the long and narrow tubercles with a white-dotted epider- mis. (Cactaceae)
For Leon Diguet (1859-1926), French chemist and explorer in Mexico. (Ferocac- tus, Fouquieria, Pereskiopsis)
Gr. 'di-', two; and Gr. 'gyne', woman; for the often double central flowers. (Portulaca)
Lat., broadened, expanded; (1) for the broad leaves. (Bijlia) - (2) for the broad sepals. (Ruschia)
For Prof. John J. Dillen [Dillenius] (1684-1747), German botanist from Darm- stadt, later in Oxford. (Opuntia)
For the occurrence in the Dil- ungu area of the Biano Plateau, Shaba Prov., Zaire. (Monadenium)
Lat., diluted; probably for the very pale pink petals. (Lampranthus)
Lat., diminutive; for the small size of the plants. (Sedum)

Gr. 'dimorphus', in two forms; and Gr. '-phyllus', -leaved. (Sedum)

Gr., in two forms; (1) for the dis- similar vegetative and flowering sterns. (Ce- ropegia) - (2) because cultivated plants are much larger. (Haworthia marumiana var.) - (3) for the differing leaves on young and old branches. (Psilocaulon) - (4) for the stellate or cage-like flowers. (Brachystelma)
For Dina Buining (fl. 1973), wife of the Dutch cactus enthusiast Albert Buining. (Arrojadoa)
For W. Dinklage (fl. 1985), horticu1- turist at the Botanical Garden Heidelberg, Germany. (Kalanchoe)
For Prof. Kurt M. Dinter (1868 -1945), German botanist famous for his ex- plorations in Namibia; and Gr. 'anthos', flower. (Aizoaceae)

Lat., dioecious. (Jatropha , Mammilla- ria, Phytolacca)

For Pedanios Dioscorides, most in- fluential Greek physician and herbalist of the first century C.E. (Dioscoreaceae)
From the old name 'Dioscoris' or 'Dioscorida' for Socotra; for the occurrence there. (Duvaliandra)
For Mrs. Di Page (fl. 1993), South African naturalist and specialist in the Swartkops Valley Bushveld vegetation. (Drosanthemum)
Perhaps the ancient oriental name for some species today elassified as Muscari ("Grape Hyacinth"). (Hyacinthaceae)
Gr. 'di-', double, two; and Gr. 'peta- lon' , petal; for the 2-petalled flowers. (Pe- largonium)

Gr. 'dischides', eleft in two; for the apically bifid staminal corona. (Asclepiada- ceae)
Gr. '-opsis', similar to; and for the genus Dischidia (Asclepiadaceae) . (Asc- lepiadaceae)
Lat. 'discus', disc; and Lat. '-for- mis', -shaped; for the shape of the plant body. (Strombocactus)
Lat. 'discus', disc; and Lat. 'cac- dissimilis tus', cactus ; for the depressed-globose to flat disc-like plant bodies. (Cactaceae)
Lat., disc-shaped; (1) for the all- disc florets. (Othonna carnosa var.) - (2) for the rounded shape of the bract cup. (Monadenium) - (3) for the shape of the corona. (Brachystelma)

Lat., many-coloured, with different colours; (1) for the spination. (Mammilla- ria) - (2) for the colour markings below each areole, (Opuntia) - (3) because of the differently coloured leaf tips. (Rhodiola) - (4) application obscure. (Pachycormus)

Lat., scattered (from Lat. 'disperg- ere', to scatter), for the scattered occurrence in contrast to the crowded populations of re- lated taxa. (Euphorbia)

Lat., dissected; for the deeply lobed leaves. (Jatropha, Kedrostis)
Lat., dissimilar; (1) for the variable flowers of the taxon. (Aspidoglossum) - (2) because the taxon has no elose affinities with any other. (Bulbine, Lampranthus, Rhipsalis) dissitispinus
Lat. 'dissitus', well-spaced, re- mote; and Lat. '<spinus', -spined. (Euphor- bia)

Lat., distichous, two-ranked; for the leaf arrangement. (Boophane, Gasteria)

Lat. 'diversus', different; and Lat. '-folius', -Ieaved. (Drosanthemum, Ha- worthia nigra var., Hoya, Ruschia, Sino- crassula, Stathmostelma , Trochomeriopsis)
Lat., 'diversus', different; and Gr. 'phyllon', leaf; for the unequal size of the leaves on a branchlet. (Cephalophyllum)
Lat., divided; application obscure. ( Gunniopsis)
Gr. 'di-', two; Gr. 'xanthos', yellow; and Gr. 'kentron', centre; for the of- ten 2 yellow central spines. (Mammillaria)

For the occurrence near Dod- oma, Central Prov., Tanzania. (Portulaca)

Lat., dragon; see Dracaena for an ex- planation of the etymology. (Dracaena)

Probably for Thomas Drum- mond (1780-1835), Scottish collector and botanist, working in N America 1831-1835. (Crassula)

For the occurrence near Pretoria, RSA. (Aloe, Ceropegia decidua ssp.)
For Paul R. Preuss (1861-?), German (Prussian) botanist and explorer in Africa, America and Asia. (Dorstenia cuspidata var.)
MLat. 'prima vera', beginning of spring (from Lat. 'ver', spring); because the taxon is the first amongst related taxa to ftower in spring. (Conophytum bolusiae ssp., Oscularia)
Lat. 'primus', the first; and Lat. 'lanatus', woolly; for the outstanding white colour of the spination. (Echinocereus)
For Mr. Richard Primos (ft. 1928- 1936), of Cape Town, Western Cape, RSA, field collector. (Ruschia, Vanheerdea)
For the genus Primula ("Prim- rose", Primulaceae) ; and Lat. '-ftorus', -ftowered; for the resemblance of the ftowers to those of some Primroses. (Para- keelya)
For the genus Primula ("Prim- rose", Primulaceae); and Lat. '-folius', -leaved; for the resemblance of the leaves to those of some Primroses. (Euphorbia)
Lat., like a Primula; for the pale yellow ftowers. (Huernia thuretii var.)
Gr. '-oides ', resembling; and for the genus Primula ("Prirnrose", Primu- laceae); perhaps for the superficially similar appearance. (Rhodiola)
Gen. of Lat. 'princeps', prince; be- cause the genus was named for the Prince of Leuchtenberg. (Leuchtenbergia)

For Cyrus G. Pringle (1838-1911), US-American plant breeder and explorer- collector. (Agave deserti ssp., Echeveria, Mammillaria rhodantha ssp., Pachycereus, Sedum, Villadia) - (2) For Victor L. Pringle (RSA) who first collected the taxon in 1973. tHaworthia decipiens var.)
For Gerry Prinsloo (ft. 1965), South African grower of succulents. (Aloe)
Gr. 'prion', saw; and Gr. 'gonia', edge; for the saw-toothed ribs. (Caralluma)
Lat., old, traditional; perhaps because the subspecies is diploid and thus evolution- arily older than the other subspecies. (Se- dum wrightii ssp.) Prometheum
Lat., prism-shaped; for the more or less three-angled leaves. (Tanquana)
For Hauptmann von Prittwitz (ft. 1907), without further data. (Kalanchoe)
For Peter R. O. Bally (1895- 1980), Swiss botanist, widely travelling in E Africa, and resident in Kenya from the 1930s. (Euphorbia)
Lat., tall, slender; (1) for the growth habit. (Alluaudia, Armatocereus, Echeveria, Parodia, Rhodiola integrifolia ssp.) - (2) for the tall inftorescences. (Aloe)
For Jaroslav Proch äzka (ft. 1995), Czech cactus hobbyist and Gymno- calycium specialist in Brno. (Gymnocalyci- um valnicekianum ssp.)
Lat., procumbent, creeping, prostrate. (Caralluma, Echinocereus pen- talophus ssp., Galenia, Huemia, Lampran- thus, Raphionacme)
Lat., extended, elongated; (1) for the long leaves. (Agave) - (2) for the long calyx lobes. (Lampranthus) - (3) for the long segments of the corona. iSchizoglos- sum bidens ssp.)
Lat., deep; for the closing bodies, which are deeply placed in the fruit locules. (Lampranthus)
Lat., spread out, profuse, abundant; for the abundantly produced ftowers and fruits. (Opuntia galapageia var.)
Lat., bulging forwards; for the an- nulus of the ftowers. (Orbea)

Lat., proliferating, producing offsets; (1) for the growth habit. (Conophytum swa- nepoelianum ssp., Crassula columnaris ssp., Cylindropuntia, Mammillaria) - (2) for the branched inftorescences with several many-ftowered part-inftorescences. Lat., prolific, fertile; for the easy propagation from leaves and bracts. (Eche- veria)
Lat., lengthened, extended; for the long branches. (Antimima)

After the Greek mythological figure Prometheus, who stole the fire from Zeus and was subsequently chained to the prominens Caucasus for punishment; for the blood-red flowers of the type species which occurs in the Caucasus. (Crassulaceae)
Lat., prominent; for the prominent and distinctive size of the plants. (Avonia)
Lat., slightly raised; for the raised dots on the leaf surface. (Lampranthus)
Gen. of Lat. 'promontorium', promontory; (1) for the occurrence at the cape of of Baja Califomia, Mexico. (Agave) - (2) for the occurrence on the Cape Penin- sula, RSA. (Erepsia, Lampranthus)
Lat., promontory; for the oc- currence on the Cape Peninsula, RSA. (Crassula capensis var.)
Lat., leaning forward, prostrate; for the growth habit. (Euphorbia)
Lat. 'propagulum', bulbi!; and Lat. '-fer, -fera, -ferum', -carrying; for the production of bulbils in the inflorescence. (Aloe)
Lat., near, neighbouring; perhaps for the simi!arity to another taxon . (Antimi- ma, Gunniopsis, Stathmostelma)
Lat., bursting forth, bursting through; application obscure. ( xGa steraloe)
Lat., 'prostratus" prostrate, creeping; and Lat. 'florus', -flowered. (Sta- pelia erectiflora var.)
Lat., prostrate, creeping; (1) for the growth habit. (Antimima, Brachystelma, Drosanthemum, Galenia, Leptocereus, Plec- tranthus) - (2) for the spreading leaves. (Aloe)

Lat. 'pruinosus', pruinose, cov- ered with a waxy bloom; and Lat. '-folius', -leaved. (Peperomia)

Lat., like a pIum or prune; for the glaucous plum-coloured leaves. (Echeveria)
For Nikolai M. Przewalski (1839- 1888), Russian soldier, traveller, geographer and naturalist, explorer of Central Asia. (Se- dum)
Gr. 'psamme, psammos', sand; and Gr. 'philos" friend; for the prefer- ence of sandy habitats. (Crassula brevifolia ssp., Euphorbia, Kedrostis, Plectranthus)
Gr. 'psamme, psammos', sand; and Gr. '-phoros" -carrying; because sand sticks to the viscid leaf surfaces. (Aizo- aceae)

Lat., little and beautiful (Dirn. of Lat. 'pulcher', beautiful); (1) for the general appearance. Lat., cushion-shaped, strongly convex; (1) for the growth forming compact cushions. (Euphorbia, Mesembryanthemum) - (2) for the cushion-like leaves. (Echeve- ria) - (3) for the thickened base of the leaves. (Sedum) - (4) für the thickened cor- olla. (Stapelia)

(Agave, Antimima, Delonix, Dudleya cymosa ssp., Frailea , Kalanchoe, Marsde- nia, Opuntia, Parakeelya, Phyllobolus, Se- della, Sempervivum) - (2) for the small (for the genus) ftowers. (Epiphyllum)
For the occurrence in the Puna vegeta- tion in Andean South America. (Talinum)

Lat., dotted (from Lat. 'punctus', sting, dot); (1) for the purplish spots along the leaf margins. (Aichryson) - (2) for the dots found on the corolla. (Alsobia, Brachy- stelma, Pelargonium, Piaranthus) - (3) per- haps for the verrucose corolla. (Dischidia)

Lat., pungent, piercing; (1) for the spination. (Cleistocactus) - (2) for the sharp-pointed leaf tips. (Haworthia) - (3) for the spines developing from sterile branches of the inftorescences. (Arenifera) - (4) for the spines developing from the pedi- cels after the fruits have fallen off . (Ru- schia)
Lat. 'puniceus', cnm son, Phoenician purple; and Lat. 'discus', disc; for the colour of the base of the inside of the ftowers. (Rhipsalis)

For William F. Purcell (186 6- 1919), English zoologi st emigrating to RSA when still a child. (Crassula atropurpurea var.)
For the botanical collector Purdom (ft. 1916), without further data. (Sedum)
For Carl Purdy (ft. 1936), possibly Carlton E. Purdy (1861 -1945 ), US-Ameri- can horticulturist, nurseryman and plant col- lector. (Sedum spathulifolium ssp.)
Lat., becoming purple; for the inside ofthe corolla. (Ceropegia) pustuligemmus
Lat., purple-red; for the colour of the lower face of the leaves. (Plectranthus)
Lat. 'purpureus' , purple; and Lat. 'albus', white; for the petals. (Cephalo- phyllum)
Lat. 'purpureus', purple; and Lat. 'croceus', saffron-yellow; for the ftower colour. (Malephora)
Lat. 'purpureus', purple; and Lat. 'fuscus' , sombre brown; for the ftower colour. (Hoya)
Lat. 'purpureus', purple; and Lat. 'stylus', style. (Cerochlamys)
Lat. 'purpureus', purple; and Lat. 'viridis', green; for the ftowering sterns often ftushed purplish. (Rhodiola)

Lat., purple; (1) for the colour of the plants. (Tetragonia) - (2) for the pur- plish leaf margins. (Aloe) - (3) for the col- our of the central spine. Lat. 'pusillus', very small, min- ute; and Lat. '-ftorus', -flowered. (Cipoce- reus, Coryphantha)

Lat., pustulate, covered with blis- ters or papillae; for the hairy leaves. (Cras- sula)

and Lat. 'gernma', bud; for the blistered sur- face of the flower buds. (Aloe)

For Mr. Puttkarner (fl. 1914), without further data. (Hereroa)

Lat., dwarf; for the small size of the plants. (Brachystelma, Ceraria, Cistan- the, Eriosyce taltalensis var., Frailea, Ha- worthia, Lewisia, Moringa, Portulaca, Reb- utia, Trichodiadema, Tylecodon)
